title: Command Me to Work for You!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source contains three deliberate boxed repetitions: the appeal to disappointed candidates, the paragraph about the ruling party's unpublished manifesto, and the assurance that a DMK Government would uphold justice rather than seek revenge. They are retained in their printed positions. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-visible salutation and repairs only unambiguous OCR defects confirmed against source pages 359-367, including the alliance phrase, `தேர்தல் அறிக்கை`, `இமயமலை`, `தந்தை பெரியார்`, and `வலுசேர்த்தது`. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: The filing of nominations for the Fifteenth Legislative Assembly election begins today, 22 April 2016. Those who submitted applications seeking to contest on behalf of the Kazhagam, appeared before the interview panel formed under my leadership, were examined by the committee and selected will now file their nomination papers. A few of the candidates selected, announced after scrutiny on behalf of the Kazhagam, have since been changed. Those who were announced earlier may naturally feel disappointed by these changes. They are not unaware of the reasons underlying them. Even so, they may feel that it would have been better not to announce their names in the first place than to announce them and later allow the opportunity to slip away. At the outset, I ask all of them to be patient and not magnify changes that had to be made because there was no other course.

Even while seeking to avoid changing candidates, the changes were made with one purpose: somehow, the Kazhagam must win this election and save the people of Tamil Nadu from Jayalalithaa's tyrannical rule. That must be kept in mind. As I have said before, in this election it is the DMK that contests under the Rising Sun symbol given by Anna, and it is M. Karunanidhi who stands as the candidate in every constituency. If you carve that thought into your heart, candidates who were changed for the common cause need not feel any sorrow!

Those who did not receive an opportunity, and those who received one and were later replaced, must work with the noble conviction that the Kazhagam's victory will itself be their victory and that they too must contribute to it. As your elder brother, I ask this of you with affection and with the right that relationship gives me.

Next, those who did not receive an opportunity to contest this election came to meet me in person and fired a volley of questions: “What wrong did we commit? Have we not worked for the Kazhagam? Why were we not selected as candidates?” I was unable to answer and console them. I could only listen patiently to all they had to say. Before the candidates were selected, a few people came and pressed their cases with me; when those they recommended did not receive an opportunity, they did not return to meet me.

To give an example, Arcot Veerasamy laboured for the Kazhagam for many long years. Indeed, he served with distinction as the Kazhagam's treasurer. He went to prison and was beaten so brutally that he lost his hearing. He sought an opportunity for his son this time, but I could not grant it. Likewise, L. Ganesan of Thanjavur helped give rise to the student movement in the Kazhagam and helped turn students into warriors of the language struggle. He too sought an opportunity for his son, but I could not grant it. The seat was not handed to some unrelated person. It was given to Dr Anjugam Boopathy, daughter of our younger brother Boopathy, who laboured continuously for the Kazhagam until his death.

In the Gandarvakottai constituency, our Kavithaipithan, celebrated as the Kazhagam's “flame of poetry” and a gifted poet, sought an opportunity. Even today he has written a poem in *Murasoli* praising me; the Kazhagam is his very breath and speech. In the same constituency, Tamil Raja, the beloved son of Keerai Tamil Selvan, who worked for the Kazhagam until his death, also sought an opportunity. Neither of them was given the seat; a new candidate has been fielded. Yet whether they receive an opportunity or not, these two, and others like them, dedicate themselves to the Kazhagam and live for the ideals Anna gave us. I have complete faith that all of them understand me very well.

Therefore, those who did not receive an opportunity, and those who received one and were later replaced, must work with the noble conviction that the Kazhagam's victory will itself be their victory and that they too must contribute to it. As your elder brother, I ask this of you with affection and with the right that relationship gives me.

In the election to be held on 16 May 2016, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is contesting together with the great Congress movement, the Indian Union Muslim League, Puthiya Tamilagam, the Manithaneya Makkal Katchi, the Tamil Nadu Farmers and Labourers Party, the Perunthalaivar Makkal Katchi, the Makkal DMDK and the Social Equality Force Party. Apart from these parties, numerous social and public-welfare movements, too many to list here, are not contesting the election and are supporting our alliance. All of them are working vigorously for the victory of our front. You know very well that even though we wished certain other parties also to be part of our front, we were not responsible for their absence!

Those in our alliance regard office as an opportunity and a responsibility to serve the people. We do not regard office as some people do, as the greatest pleasure of all, a rosewater bath, a drink of fruit juice or the fulfilment of one's very birth.

We use office only as a democratic instrument to work for you, serve you, strive for you and perform a duty of service for you. Nothing more. Once, when Arcot Veerasamy, district secretary Anbazhagan and I set out for election campaigning in Chennai, reporters surrounded me and asked: “Jayalalithaa keeps announcing freebies. She announced one yesterday and another today. What free gift are you going to announce?”
I replied: “I have nothing to announce as a free gift. I alone am here. I announce myself, free of charge, to the people of Tamil Nadu.”
I said it with the confidence that nobody but me could come forward and compete with me in offering this free gift.

Even now, the ruling party has not issued its election manifesto. We hear that it has enlisted a few officials to examine every scheme announced in the manifestos of other parties, add still more to them, and attempt to dazzle the people with illusion and magic. But the people are not prepared to trust either them or their hollow, clamorous announcements. Our people know from experience whose manifesto will be implemented and who will do what they promise.

During the past five years of AIADMK rule, has any section of Tamil Nadu lived in peace? Even the ruling party's ministers, though they amassed wealth in crores, were they themselves allowed to live in peace? Is the person who stands second in the Cabinet, and who twice served as Chief Minister, able to live without fear under this Government?

What became of the person who ranked third in the Cabinet? He was responsible for the Electricity Department and was also the minister for Prohibition and Excise. He is said to have accumulated assets, here and abroad, of so many crores that they cannot even be counted. He was not permitted to contest from his own constituency. Were some ministers not denied seats altogether, while three others appeared on the list of changed candidates? If this is the fate of ministers, need one even speak of everyone else? There is a proverb: “If it holds its shape, it is Pillaiyar; if not, it is merely clay.” That party is the very embodiment of the proverb!

The ruling party has yet to publish its election manifesto. We hear that it has enlisted a few officials to examine every scheme announced in the manifestos of other parties, add still more to them, and attempt to dazzle the people with illusion and magic. But the people are not prepared to trust either them or their hollow, clamorous announcements. Our people know from experience whose manifesto will be implemented and who will do what they promise.

Thirteen thousand Makkal Nala Paniyalargal, people's welfare workers, were appointed under the DMK Government. Merely because of that, this Government's heart did not soften even when the court permitted their reinstatement. How many of them took their own lives? What consolation can Jayalalithaa offer for the tears shed by the women in those families?

Has any government anywhere in India ever attracted as much condemnation from the courts as this one? How many officials have been fined by the courts? Is there a government here with enough feeling even to care about it?

How many people were subjected to fabricated, vindictive cases by this Government merely because they had served in the Kazhagam Cabinet? How many were charged in property cases? After they spent five years climbing the long steps of the courts, judgments in their cases have only now begun to go against this Government.

Consider the Police Department! What cruelties have been inflicted over the past five years merely because certain officers held important responsibilities under the DMK Government? ADGP Jaffer Sait held an important post in the intelligence wing during the Kazhagam administration. When the Government changed in 2011, he was transferred to the Mandapam refugee camp. He was accused of improperly obtaining a Housing Board plot, and the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption police searched eight places, including the homes of his wife and daughter. He was then suspended with effect from 1 August 2011. Seeking to have the suspension quashed, he approached the Central Administrative Tribunal in Chennai. A Bench comprising Justice A. Arumughaswamy and Administrative Member R. Ramanujam yesterday set aside the Tamil Nadu Government's suspension order and directed that Jaffer Sait be restored to duty.

Like him, an IPS officer named Thukkaiyandi was suspended on the day he was due to retire. He renounced worldly life and went away to the foothills of the Himalayas. Another officer, Jangid, an IPS officer of ADGP rank, was humiliated by being kept in a minor post in Kanyakumari district for five years. Even when he appealed directly to the Chief Secretary that his wife was unwell, this Government showed no compassion. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa now calls herself a mother. What happened to Archana Ramasundaram, a woman herself, and why? To what extent did the IPS officer Sathyapriya suffer under this Government?

When a case was registered in Salem district against the late former minister Veerapandi Arumugam, the Government claimed that the accused had not been arrested immediately. It transferred fifty-four police personnel, including fifteen inspectors, sixteen sub-inspectors and twenty-three head constables, from Salem to Madurai, Tirunelveli and Ramanathapuram districts. Have we forgotten that this Government's actions also contributed to the death of our younger brother Veerapandi Arumugam?

The people need liberation from these authoritarian cruelties. Justice needs liberation. Fairness and integrity need freedom. That is why we are now waging and labouring in a second freedom struggle. Seeing us struggle and work in this way, some fear that if we win this election and form the Government, we will take revenge. If we form the Government, justice and fairness alone will always prevail. Government will not bend to our likes and dislikes. It will be government according to law. We will not roam about in a frenzy seeking revenge against anyone.

I am not a person disposed to seek revenge against anyone. Unlike Jayalalithaa, I do not possess a vindictive nature. Father Periyar shaped me into a person of love and humanity. Arignar Anna enabled me to walk through society with my head held high and honed me for political life. The Tamil people refined me and made me sharper still. The Tamil language I learned gave me strength!

Meanwhile, I begin my tour tomorrow to campaign and to meet all of you in person and seek your votes. I know what you are asking: “Do you not know your own age? Do we not understand the state of your health? Must you strain yourself and come in person?”

Some fear that if we win this election and form the Government, we will take revenge. If we form the Government, justice and fairness alone will always prevail. Government will not bend to our likes and dislikes. It will be government according to law. We will not roam about in a frenzy seeking revenge against anyone.

I am not a person disposed to seek revenge against anyone. Unlike Jayalalithaa, I do not possess a vindictive nature. Father Periyar shaped me into a person of love and humanity. Arignar Anna enabled me to walk through society with my head held high and honed me for political life. The Tamil people refined me and made me sharper still. The Tamil language I learned gave me strength!

I know you are asking all this. Even so, some among you will say: “Our elder brother has not come. In how many elections has he arrived even at midnight and spoken for an hour or an hour and a half? Once at Krishnagiri and once at Thoothukudi, his campaign continued until daybreak, until the sun rose. He has travelled after eating curd rice in the van along the way.” My health may not cooperate to the extent I desire or match the enthusiasm overflowing in my heart. Yet I have decided to tour as far as it permits, meet our people in person and seek their votes. Therefore, rather than expecting me to climb a stage in every town, you must allow me to speak from the van, and to speak only briefly. The public too must graciously bear with me!

I generally “dictate” detailed answers to the questions Chief Minister Jayalalithaa directs at me, both through my *Udanpirappu* letters and through the question-and-answer column. Take all those replies to the people through doorstep campaigning! Very few days remain before the election. Within that time, I shall try to visit every town I can. The entire responsibility for making my journey a success rests with you.

My beloved Udanpirappugal, dearer to me than life itself, command me to work for you, to continue labouring for the Tamil people who are mingled with my very flesh! Victory is ours! Support the Rising Sun!
With affection,
M.K.
22 April 2016
